rulings.Case Title: TYRONE L. ADAMS VS. CHARLES L. EASLEY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Joinder In Motion To Lift Stay
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 28, 2019, Line 1, 2 - DEFENDANT ATTORNEY JOHN JUDSON WAGGOMAN's (SUED AS JOHN JUDSON WAGGOMAN, A PROFESSIONAL LAW CORPORATION) Joinder In Motion To Lift Stay. Defendants' motion to lift stay and abatement is granted. While Cal. R. Ct. 3.515 does not apply here, the Court has the power to grant the motion in light of its inherent power to "amend and control its process and orders so as to make them conform to law and justice." (Code Civ. Proc. § 128(a)(8).) On June 2, 2016, this court granted a stay in this case pending final resolution of case No. C081016 in the Third District Court of Appeal. The Third District has finally resolved that appeal. On May 23, 2019, the Third District held that the June 25, 2013 judgment Plaintiff was seeking to appeal was final and not appealable and modified the award of attorney fees and costs. (Perkins Decl. ISO Mt. to Lift, Ex. E.) On June 19, 2019, the court denied Plaintiff's petition for rehearing, and the court has since issued its remittitur. (Id., Ex. F.) Because the original reason for a stay no longer exists, the stay should be lifted. That Defendant Buttacavoli Industries is protected by an automatic stay pursuant to Bankr. Code § 362 is of no consequence. "In the absence of special circumstances, stays pursuant to section 362(a) are limited to debtors and do not include non-bankrupt co-defendants." (Ingersoll-Rand Financial v. Miller Min. (9th Cir. 1987) 817 F.2d 1424, 1427.) One situation in which courts have found special circumstances is when the party protected by the bankruptcy stay is "an indispensable party" such that "the litigation cannot proceed in his absence and therefore must be stayed as against the third party." (In re James Wilson Assoc. (7th Cir. 1992) 965 F.2d 160, 170.) Plaintiff has not shown that litigation in this matter cannot proceed without Defendant Buttacavoli Industries. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to submit a proposed order for signature and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Join Douglass Manor Homeowners Association Inc., As A Plaintiff And A Cross-Defendant And/Or Defendant
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 28, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF STEPHEN KAVANAUGH's Motion To Join Douglass Manor Homeowners Association Inc., As A Plaintiff And A Cross-Defendant And/Or Defendant. Plaintiff Stephen Kavanaugh is granted leave to amend the complaint in order to add Douglass Manor Homeowner's Association, Inc. as a defendant. As Defendant Masterson concedes, the Court has broad discretion to grant leave to amend the pleadings. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 473(a)(1).) Trial is not until August 2020 and there is no prejudice to Defendants at this time. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiff Kavanaugh is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Demas Yan Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 28, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT DEMAS YAN's Motion To Tax Costs. Defendant Demas Yan's motion to tax costs is denied. Defendant has not met his burden to show that the costs claimed were not actually or reasonably incurred. (See Ladas v. California State Auto. Assn. (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 774 ["If the items appearing in a cost bill appear to be proper charges, the burden is on the party seeking to tax costs to show that they were not reasonable or necessary."].) The Fu declaration provides a sufficient showing for the Court to conclude that they were. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiffs are required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. VS. JENNA K LARSEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 28, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'s Motion For Summary Judgment. Plaintiff Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'s motion for summary judgment is granted. Plaintiff is entitled to the principal of $8,582.45 and costs of $815 for a total judgment of $9,397.45. Plaintiff has maintained its burden of production on each of its six breach of contract and common counts claims based upon plaintiff's deposition testimony and the declaration of plaintiff's qualified witness (Carol Rowe). The court overrules defendant's objections to the Rowe declaration. Ms. Rowe properly lays her foundation to testify regarding the business records that meet the business records exception of Evidence Code, § 1271. (Rowe Dec., 1-6; People v. Dorsey (1974) 43 Cal.App.3d 953, 960 ["[W]e believe that bank statements prepared in the regular course of banking business and in accordance with banking regulations are in a different category than the ordinary business and financial records of a private enterprise."]; Evid. Code, § 1552(a) ["A printed representation of computer information or a computer program is presumed to be an accurate representation of the computer information or computer program that it purports to represent."]) Ms. Rowe establishes that: (1) the documents were made in the ordinary course of business (Rowe Dec., 4, 11, 12, and 13); (2) the documents were made at or near the time, act, condition or event (id. 4, 12, and 14); (3) the identity and mode of preparation of the documents is provided (id. 4, 5, and 12); and (4) the sources of information and method of preparation are trustworthy. (Id. 4, 5, and 12.) These documents demonstrate how much is owed on the account. Defendant's reliance on People v. Sanchez (2016) 63 Cal.4th 665 is misplaced as the instant case does not implicate expert testimony. This is a simple collection action involving the business records exception to the hearsay rule. Defendant's deposition testimony demonstrates that: (1) she had one credit card account with plaintiff (Larsen Depo., 9:12-10:1); (2) she admits that she owes money on the account but is unsure regarding the amount (id. at 11:15-12:6); and (3) she received billing statements from plaintiff, reviewed them, never contested the balance or late fees, and the only reason she stopped making payments was because she could not afford them. (Id. at 14:1-15:18.) In opposition, defendant declares that she does not owe any money or she owes less and she did not agree to the adduced interest rates or late fees. This declaration contradicts the above testimony and is excluded. "A party cannot create an issue of fact by a declaration which contradicts his prior pleadings. [Citation.] In determining whether any triable issue of material fact exists, the trial court may, in its discretion, give great weight to admissions made in deposition and disregard contradictory and self-serving affidavits of the party." (Preach v. Monter Rainbow (1993) 12 Cal.App.4th 1441, 1451.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JEFFREY MA ET AL VS. PATRICK FOY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories And To Impose Monetary Sanctions On Defendant North Point Investments Partners, Llc
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 28, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF MICHAEL LUU'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories And To Impose Monetary Sanctions On Defendant North Point Investments Partners, Llc Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. Moving Party should be prepared to address the issue of moving party's separate statement which does not appear to properly comply with CRC 3.1345(c); Mills v. U.S. Bank (2008) 166 Cal. App. 4th 871. Moving party should also be prepared to address how the moving party has complied with C.C.P. § 2016.040. The declaration submitted in support of the motion does not appear to show a reasonable and good faith meet and confer effort on the part of the moving party. The court is inclined to deny the motion and award sanctions in favor of Defendant and against moving party pursuant to C.C.P. §§ 2023.010(i), 2023.020. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to williamclynn@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LUKE ERCEG ET AL VS. MARINELLI SHELLFISH SF ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Sam'S Chowder House, Lp'S Motion To Strike Complaint (For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 28, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT SAM'S CHOWDER HOUSE, LP's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. (Part 2 of 2) Plaintiffs' cases are distinguishable. Mexicali Rose v. Superior Court (1992) 1 Cal.4th 617, did not address the availability of punitive damages in a food consumption product liability action; moreover, the request for punitive damages there was based on the allegation defendants initially refused to obtain medical assistance for plaintiff when he sustained throat injuries as a result of swallowing a chicken bone contained in an enchilada he ordered at defendants' restaurant. (Id. at 620.) Da Silva v. Pacific King, Inc. (1987) 195 Cal.App.3d 1, a Jones Act and maritime action, held that under the circumstances presented there, any violation of the regulation on which plaintiff's negligence per se claim was based "would not support a finding of 'the conscious disregard for the safety of others' which is necessary for punitive damages." (Id. at 7.) In any event, it predated the 1987 amendments to Civil Code section 3294, which, "[f]or plaintiffs attempting to prove malice by showing a 'conscious disregard' of their rights as opposed to an actual intent to harm, . . . imposed additional requirements of 'despicable' and 'willful' defense conduct." (College Hospital, Inc., 8 Cal.4th at 713.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of tentative ruling.) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LUKE ERCEG ET AL VS. MARINELLI SHELLFISH SF ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, October 28, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT SAM'S CHOWDER HOUSE, LP's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2) Defendant Sam's Chowder House, LP's motion to strike prayer for punitive damages is granted. In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) The allegations in the complaint that Defendant Sam's Chowder House, LP failed to confirm that the oysters it served were fit for human consumption, failed to properly instruct its staff on the handling of seafood, and that it "knew or should have known" of the resulting risks (Compl. 29) are classic negligence allegations that do not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). Plaintiffs' allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171; see Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-10 [trial court properly granted motion for summary adjudication of punitive damages where no reasonable juror could find that defendant's conduct was despicable]; see also Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].) (Continued to Part 2) =(302/EPS)
